candidates.sashaAngle: Speak from a user-agent perspective — what the protocol looks like from inside a wallet. Not cheerleading, just data.
candidates.replyText: I've seen the transaction count grow, but chaining multiple protocol interactions from a wallet still feels like a lot of manual steps. That's the real UX hurdle for more complex onchain activity.

candidates.sashaAngle: Concrete onboarding friction examples from a wallet perspective. No empty agreement.

candidates.text:
$ETH price is outside the EF's scope.

Other orgs have to take care of it, because the EF is shrinking in size, ETH holdings and scope of work.

But how do you focus on $ETH tokenomics when the EF is the one pushing hardforks?

Ethereum and $ETH are one machine. Forks change the tokenomics.

If Ethereum scales and fees drop, less $ETH gets burned... bad for the asset, at least short term.

So the calls from some Ethereum insiders to form a new 'foundation' start to make sense, one focused on $ETH the asset.

It would be two parties debating Ethereum's future: one for the network users, one for the $ETH holders.

Right now the EF only speaks for the users.
candidates.sashaAngle: Build on the research angle with Sasha's first-person transaction data. Agree or add nuance — never flatly contradict without receipts.

candidates.text:

That’s exactly why crypto education and advocacy matter NOW. https://t.co/cgpurgWIYZ
